retrieved_docs.title: March Fracture (Metatarsal Stress Fractures)(Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Metatarsal stress fractures are common in athletes and the military, accounting for 25% of all stress fractures and 20% of all sports medicine clinic visits. [5] [7] Forty percent of all athletes will suffer a stress fracture at some point in their careers. [8] Overall data suggest that females have a higher incidence of march fractures than men. In addition, those with prior stress fractures are more likely to develop another. Sixty percent of patients with a stress fracture will have had a previous one. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Bimalleolar Ankle Fractures -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ankle fractures account for 9% of all fractures. In the United States, ankle fractures are the most common lower limb fractures and are the most frequent fracture or injury in the emergency room. The bimalleolar fracture accounts for 60% of all ankle fractures, with an incidence of 187 fractures per 100,000 people. This fracture has a bimodal distribution and most commonly affects older women and young males. Ankle fracture is the third most common fracture overall; in athletes, it is the most frequently encountered fracture. Also, this is the third most frequent fracture in patients over 60. [3] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Smith Fracture Review -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The Danish National Patient Registry records show a 31% increase in fractures among adult males from 1997 to 2018, mostly driven by the increasing population of older individuals. Increased surgical fixation rates have also been observed within this period, also attributed to the growing number of older patients. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: March Fracture (Metatarsal Stress Fractures)(Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: March fractures are best managed by an interprofessional team that includes nurses and physical therapists. The key is prevention. Patients need to be educated on the importance of stretching before exercise. In addition, correcting predisposing biomechanical conditions, such as gait training and arch supports, can be beneficial. The outcomes for most patients are good, but recurrence is common. Failure to diagnose a march fracture can lead to non-union and moderate disability.

retrieved_docs.title: March Fracture (Metatarsal Stress Fractures)(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: During an interview, patients may describe an insidious, new onset of pain that improves transiently with rest but increases again with activity. Questions should focus on new exercise routines or routines that may have increased in intensity, duration, or frequency over the last 6 to 8 weeks. Changes in terrain or shoe gear should also be noted, as these may elucidate changes in force distributions through the foot. [4] Pain may be nonspecific in location but is often described as dull, aching, and worse with any weight-bearing activities. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Acetabular Fractures -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Acetabular fractures primarily occur in young people who are involved in high-velocity trauma.  Since the advent of mandatory seatbelt use, there has been a significant reduction of acetabular fractures to an approximate incidence of 3 per 100000.  There has been an increase in the number of acetabulum fractures resulting from a fall of fewer than 10 feet, likely due to the rise in osteopenia/osteoporosis. [1] Little has changed since Letournel and Judet’s landmark paper in 1993, and many of their findings remain the “gold standard” for treatment today.  Among the most significant advancements has been the advent of percutaneous fixation of certain fracture types.

retrieved_docs.title: March Fracture (Metatarsal Stress Fractures)(Archived) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: March fractures are small, nondisplaced bone breaks that occur in the metatarsals of the feet. Various causes include overuse, overtraining, incorrect shoes, pedal deformities such as pes planus and overpronation, osteopenia/osteoporosis, and incorrect training habits or surfaces. When left untreated, a complete fracture can occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Femoral Shaft Fractures -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The worldwide incidence of femoral shaft fractures ranges between 10 and 21 per 100,000 per year. Two percent of these fractures are open fractures. [13] [14] The rate of atypical femur fractures, as defined by the American Society for Bone and Mineral Research Task Force 2013, ranges between 3.5% to 16%. [12] FSFs demonstrate a bimodal distribution. Men are more likely to sustain a fracture between the ages of 15 to 35, while women begin to show a steady increase starting at age 60. Men are more likely to sustain FSFs from automobile accidents or other high-energy mechanisms. Women are more likely to sustain an FSF from ground-level falls. Automobile accidents are more prevalent in the younger population, while ground-level falls are more common in the elderly population, which is attributed to osteoporosis. [13] [11] Interestingly, the age of presentation with a femoral shaft fracture has increased with time. According to the Edinburgh Orthopedic Trauma Unit in the United Kingdom, the average age of presentation in 1990 was 44, with a steady increase to 65 years in 2000, likely due to more rigorous traffic laws and improved automobile safety. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Greenstick Fracture -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 12% of all pediatric emergency department visits in the United States are due to musculoskeletal injuries. Fractures make up a large percentage of musculoskeletal injuries resulting in significant morbidity and complications. Greenstick fractures are most likely to be found in the pediatric population under 10 years of age but can occur in any age group, including adults. [3] There is an equal incidence rate in female and male patients; however, male patients are more likely to sustain fractures. [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: March Fracture (Metatarsal Stress Fractures)(Archived) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Key facts regarding march fractures are listed below. The etiology of march fractures (metatarsal stress fractures) is multifactorial, and extrinsic and intrinsic factors should be evaluated. Radiographic evidence of a stress fracture may be negative for 2 to 4 weeks after symptom onset. In most cases, conservative care, including analgesia and rest, are the only indicated options.

retrieved_docs.title: March Fracture (Metatarsal Stress Fractures)(Archived) -- Pathophysiology -- Classification Systems
retrieved_docs.content: Stress fractures can fall into two broad categories: high-risk and low-risk injuries. Stress fractures of the metatarsals are considered low-risk fractures because they are common and tend to heal well with activity modification while weight-bearing. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: March Fracture (Metatarsal Stress Fractures)(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: March fractures, ie, metatarsal stress fractures, were first described in 1855, after the foot pain and swelling experienced by Prussian soldiers on long marches. [1] March fractures are metatarsal fractures caused by repetitive stress. [2] Intrinsic patient and extrinsic environmental risk factors contribute to the development of these fractures. A combination of historical features and physical evaluation with imaging can help make the diagnosis. However, prodromal symptoms are common before evidence of a stress fracture is seen on plain radiographs. Radiographs may be negative for 2 to 4 weeks after the onset of symptoms. [3] These stress fractures are typically managed conservatively but can be complicated by nonunion. In such instances, surgical fixation may be indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: 5th Metatarsal Fracture -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Classified by Lawrence and Bottle, the base, or proximal aspect, of the fifth metatarsal is broken up into three anatomical zones: zone 1, the tuberosity; zone 2, the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction; and zone 3, the diaphyseal area within 1.5 cm of the tuberosity. [1] Fractures through zone 1 are called pseudo-Jones fractures, and fractures through zone 2 are referred to as Jones fractures. Additionally, a patient may sustain a shaft fracture greater than 1.5 cm distal to the tuberosity, a long spiral fracture extending into the distal metaphyseal area, the so-called dancer's fracture, or a stress fracture of the metatarsal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 261
retrieved_docs.content: and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. By connecting specific fracture entities, a comprehensive set of internal‑fixation and open‑reduction techniques, and hierarchical procedural categories to underlying bone‑density disorders, the knowledge base supports integrated treatment planning for orthopedic injuries of the hip, lower extremity, and shoulder‑arm region, while highlighting the importance of managing osteoporosis to reduce fracture risk and optimize surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2097
retrieved_docs.content: Fractures and dislocations of the limbs (كسور وانخلاعات الأطراف) represent a broad category of musculoskeletal injuries that includes hand fractures (كسر اليد). Hand fractures are a specific subset of upper‑limb fractures (كسر الطرف العلوي), and within this subset the injury of a broken wrist bone (عظم رسغ مكسور) is a more precise diagnosis. Consequently, a broken wrist bone is both a hand fracture and an upper‑limb fracture, all of which fall under the overarching classification of limb fractures and dislocations. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians accurately diagnose, code, and manage extremity injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 203
retrieved_docs.content: of closed fractures and various fixation techniques is tightly connected to osteoporosis‑related bone fragility. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient assessment, informed surgical planning, and coordinated management of fractures and bone‑density disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 236
retrieved_docs.content: as both a broken wrist bone and a closed distal‑forearm fracture, linking general unclassified injury categories to precise anatomical fracture types. These interrelationships underscore how vascular complications, self‑inflicted wounds, and a wide spectrum of musculoskeletal trauma—particularly fractures of the clavicle, upper and lower limbs, and associated injuries from falls or traffic accidents—are interconnected, informing accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 1186
retrieved_docs.content: Closed fracture of intracapsular section of femur is mapped to fracture of neck of femur (hip), indicating that this specific intracapsular injury is a form of hip neck fracture. The category fracture of neck of femur (hip) classifies itself as a hip neck fracture and has هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ) (osteoporosis/low bone density) as a member, reflecting the strong association between reduced bone quality and neck‑of‑femur fractures. Within the هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ) grouping, إعانة رأس الفخذ (hip‑head support) and كسور متعددة (multiple fractures) are listed as members, showing that bone‑density disorders encompass both therapeutic interventions such as hip‑head support and clinical presentations like multiple fractures. Together, these relationships illustrate how a closed intracapsular femur fracture fits into the broader classification of hip neck fractures, how osteoporosis/low bone density underlies these injuries, and how related entities such as hip‑head support procedures and the occurrence of multiple fractures are integrated within the same disease framework, informing comprehensive patient evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2111
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical vertebra fracture (كسر في الفقرة الرقبية) is classified within the broader category of other fractures, and it frequently co‑occurs with pelvic fracture (كسر الحوض). Recognizing that a cervical vertebra fracture belongs to the group of other fractures and that it often appears alongside a pelvic fracture can guide clinicians to assess for additional injuries when either injury is identified, thereby improving comprehensive patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: relationships form a structured network in which fractures—whether of multiple bones, the spine, trunk, limbs, femoral neck, or skull vault—are intricately linked to specific late‑effect conditions, anatomical locations, treatment morphologies, broader injury classifications, and associated behavioral factors, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding fracture sequelae and related injuries in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment modality for several specific orthopedic injuries. It is considered a type of treatment for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, for fractures or dislocations of the lower extremity excluding the hip and femur, and for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna. In each of these contexts, the procedure involves surgically stabilizing the bone without performing a reduction of the fracture, thereby providing a consistent therapeutic approach across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 139
retrieved_docs.content: crash, is associated with extensive tissue damage, reinforcing the link between high‑energy fractures, tissue injury, and neurotoxic effects. Overall, fracture (كسر) integrates late sequelae, specific anatomical fracture types, associated tissue damage, co‑occurring injuries, procedural interventions, and psychosocial classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 182
retrieved_docs.content: femoral bone fracture (كسر عظم الفخذ), traffic accidents, and shoulder fracture (كسر الكتف). Shoulder fractures frequently accompany head injury trauma, emphasizing the need to assess upper‑extremity injuries in patients with cranial trauma. Lower‑limb fractures may co‑occur with upper‑limb fractures, reflecting polytrauma patterns. Traffic accidents typically present with a constellation of chest injury trauma, head injury trauma, shoulder pain (وجع في الكتف), and lower‑limb fractures. Overall, the knowledge base delineates a structured hierarchy of fracture types, their anatomical locations, and common co‑occurring traumatic and vascular conditions, underscoring the importance of integrated assessment and management of limb injuries, systemic vascular disease, and associated injury circumstances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 791
retrieved_docs.content: The classification of bone injuries places كسر في الأطراف (fracture in the limbs) as the broad category for all limb fractures. Within this category, كسر الطرف السفلي (fracture of the lower limb) is identified as a direct subcategory. The English term fracture of lower limb is linked to كسر الطرف السفلي as a child concept and is further defined as كسر جزء غير محدد من عنق الفخذ، مغلق (a closed fracture of an unspecified portion of the femoral neck), indicating that this specific injury is a type of lower‑limb fracture. Additionally, كسر الكاحل (ankle fracture) is recognized as a child of كسر الطرف السفلي, illustrating that ankle fractures are a subset of lower‑limb fractures. This hierarchical structure clarifies how general limb fracture categories narrow down to specific injuries such as closed femoral‑neck fractures and ankle fractures, aiding clinicians in organizing diagnoses and treatment plans.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: of the hip and femur, lower extremities (other than hip or femur), and radius and ulna, and is synonymous with internal fixation of fracture (تثبيت داخلي للكسر). This procedure is further specified as internal fixation of the clavicle without fracture reduction and is included in the osteoporosis SMQ, highlighting its relevance in patients with compromised bone quality. Overall, the knowledge base integrates the anatomical focus on the clavicle within shoulder‑and‑arm surgical repair, connects closed upper‑limb and hip‑neck fractures to osteoporosis, and organizes a comprehensive suite of internal fixation and open‑reduction techniques for clavicular, humeral, radial, ulnar, and scapular injuries, supporting clinicians in recognizing bone‑density risk factors and selecting appropriate operative management across related anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 203
retrieved_docs.content: داخلي للكسر) is likewise a member of the osteoporosis/low‑bone‑density group and is applied across diverse skeletal sites, including the clavicle, hip, lower extremities (other than hip or femur), and the radius and ulna. Procedures and conditions related to osteoporosis extend beyond fracture fixation; hip‑head prosthesis (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are also members of the osteoporosis SMQ, illustrating how severe bone‑density loss can lead to joint replacement and recurrent fractures. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that clavicular and shoulder‑arm surgical repairs are precisely linked to shoulder anatomy, that femoral intracapsular fractures correspond to hip neck fractures and are described in multiple languages, and that the classification of closed fractures and various fixation techniques is tightly connected to osteoporosis‑related bone fragility. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient assessment, informed surgical

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 261
retrieved_docs.content: clavicle, femur, humerus, radius, scapula, and ulna—to the systemic disease category of osteoporosis or low bone density. “Fracture of neck of femur (hip)” itself is classified within the same osteoporosis SMQ, underscoring low bone mass as a key risk factor. Internal‑fixation procedures (identified as “تثبيت داخلي للكسر”) are associated with multiple treatment categories: “fracture or dislocation of hip and femur,” “fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur),” and “fracture or dislocation of radius and ulna.” These associations emphasize that bone quality influences surgical decision‑making for both hip and peripheral limb injuries. Within the osteoporosis SMQ, additional members such as “جراحة مفصل الورك” (hip joint surgery), “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” (hip head prosthesis), and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. By connecting specific fracture
